blurb: Clouds of tiny yellow flowers on bare branches in earliest spring, then edible red fruit — a tough, early-feeding dogwood.
sun: Full sun to part shade; best bloom in full sun
spread: Multi-stem clump; may produce occasional suckers — easy to control
seasons: Clusters of tiny yellow flowers on bare stems late March–early April; glossy green summer foliage; olive-shaped red 'cherries' ripen mid-summer; orange-red to purple fall color; handsome multi-stem form in winter
wildlife: Among the earliest yellow flowers of spring — a critical early pollen source for native bees and honey bees before most trees leaf out; ripening fruit eaten by birds and squirrels
deer: Generally deer-resistant; leaves and twigs may be browsed lightly but not a preferred target
toxic: No known toxicity; ripe fruit flesh is edible, pit is not eaten
origin: Central & southern Europe to western Asia (Turkey, Caucasus)
habitat: Woodland edges & scrub on limestone soils

care.hardiness: USDA zones 4–8 — reliably hardy well below Front Range winters (zones 5b–6a). It endures temperatures to about −25°C (−13°F) without protection, so even our coldest years pose no threat.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant in spring (April–May) or early fall (September) — the standard windows for woody shrubs on the Front Range. Dig the hole twice as wide as the container and equal depth; backfill with the native soil (cornelian cherry is exceptionally adaptable and does not need rich amendment, though compost addition on heavy clay speeds establishment). Water deeply at planting and again every 7–10 days for the first growing season. Bare-root specimens transplant well in early spring while dormant. Container stock can also be planted, though cornelian cherry grows large — site it where it has room (15+ ft clearance) from the start. In a large container (≥20 gal) it grows well through the season, but its roots are far less cold-hardy than the crown; overwinter the pot in an unheated garage or sink it in the ground, then plant out the following spring.[1][3]
care.sun: Best bloom and fruit production in full sun (6+ hours). Tolerates part shade but may flower less freely and set smaller fruit crops. On the Front Range's intense high-altitude sun, afternoon shade on a hot south wall is fine — the plant is adaptable.[1]
care.soil: Exceptionally tolerant of a wide pH range — acid, neutral, and alkaline soils (including pH well above 8.0) — making it one of the most pH-adaptable woody shrubs for Boulder's alkaline clay. Grows in clay, loam, and sandy soils; good drainage preferred but it tolerates brief wet periods. A generous initial planting hole on compacted clay, plus a 3–4 inch mulch layer, gives roots the best start.[1][2]
care.water: Moderate moisture during establishment (first 1–2 seasons); once established, cornelian cherry is genuinely drought-tolerant and adapts well to Colorado's semi-arid conditions without supplemental irrigation beyond natural rainfall in most years. During the first summer, water deeply every 7–10 days; by year three, established plants handle typical Front Range dry spells without stress.[1][2]
care.spacing: Allow 15–20 ft all around — this is a large multi-stem shrub or small tree that eventually reaches 15–25 ft tall and nearly as wide. For a fruiting planting, space two cultivars 15 ft apart (cross-pollination significantly improves fruit set). As a specimen or hedge screen, single plants work, but yield is better with a companion.[1][3]
care.bloom: Yellow flowers appear in clusters on bare branches in late March to early April on the Front Range — typically one of the first three or four woody plants to flower, before forsythia and well before most ornamentals. The individual blooms are tiny (less than ¼ inch) but massed in dense axillary clusters along every twig, creating a soft golden haze. Bloom can last 3–4 weeks and is the most important early pollen event for bees in this guide: in many spring years it is the first abundant pollen available when temperatures reliably reach 50°F and native bees begin foraging.[1]
care.feeding: Light feeding only. A spring topdressing of balanced slow-release fertilizer (10-10-10) or well-composted manure in early April keeps growth vigorous on lean clay. Avoid excess nitrogen — this plant fruits prolifically without pushing it, and heavy nitrogen favors leaves over flowers and fruit.[1]
care.maintenance: Minimal pruning needed. Remove dead or crossing branches in late winter or early spring before bud break. If grown as a multi-stem large shrub (the natural form), simply thin the occasional wayward branch. If a more tree-like form is desired, select 2–3 strong main stems and remove competing basal growth early. Remove suckers promptly when they appear. No regular renewal pruning is required — this is a long-lived, slow-growing plant that shapes itself over decades.[1]
care.selfsow: Produces fruit prolifically in most years; birds and squirrels distribute the pits. Occasional seedlings may appear nearby. Remove unwanted seedlings while small — they develop a strong taproot quickly. Fruit production is significantly improved by planting two different cultivars or seedling-raised plants nearby for cross-pollination; a lone plant may set some fruit but yields more generously with a companion.[1][3]
care.propagation: By seed: collect ripe red fruit in August, macerate and rinse out the pits, then cold-stratify at 34–40°F for 3–4 months before spring sowing; note that germination can be slow (sometimes 18 months or more). Vegetatively: semi-hardwood cuttings taken in July–August, treated with rooting hormone and kept in a humid frame, succeed reliably; hardwood cuttings taken in autumn also root. Layering a low branch in June–July (wound, pin to soil, leave 9 months) produces a rooted section for separation the following spring. Grafting is used commercially to produce named cultivars.[1][2]
care.harvest: Fruit ripens in August (mid-summer) on the Front Range. Pick when the olive-shaped drupes turn deep red and yield slightly to pressure — unripe fruit is sharply astringent. The ripe flesh is juicy and tart, described as having a plum-like flavor when fully soft. Use for jelly, jam, syrup, juice, or liqueur; the fruit can also be dried. The single, hard pit inside each drupe is not eaten. Process promptly after harvest — ripe fruit softens quickly. Birds will compete for the ripening berries; harvest when color is full.[1][2]
care.companions: Excellent as an understory or edge shrub near larger trees. Pairs well with other early spring performers: cornelian cherry's yellow bloom complements the catkins of pussy willow and the early flowers of mock orange. In summer, its glossy dark-green canopy provides a handsome backdrop for shasta daisies, coreopsis, or salvia. As a fruiting shrub, combine with golden currant and wood's rose in a mixed wildlife hedge.[1][3]

edible.summary: The ripe red fruit is edible and long-used in European and Middle Eastern cuisine — tart and juicy when fully soft, best cooked into jelly, jam, syrup, or juice; the pit is not eaten.[1][2]
edible.parts: The edible part is the ripe fruit flesh (mesocarp) of the olive-shaped drupe, which turns deep red and softens in mid-summer (August on the Front Range). The flavor is tart and pleasantly acidic — described as having a plum-like quality when fully ripe. Cornelian cherries have a very long history of use in Middle Eastern and European cooking: jams, jellies, syrups, pickles, liqueurs, and dried fruit are all traditional preparations. The large, hard pit (stone) inside each drupe is not eaten.[1][2]
edible.preparation: Pick fully ripe, deeply red, slightly yielding fruit. Cook: simmer with water and sugar, strain through a jelly bag for clear jelly or press for jam; the tart flavor concentrates beautifully. Juice by macerating ripe fruit and straining. For liqueur, steep in vodka or grappa with sugar. Fruit can also be dried whole and reconstituted. Unripe fruit is sharply astringent and should not be eaten raw — ripeness is critical. No toxic compounds are present in ripe fruit; no special detoxification is needed beyond cooking.[1][2]
edible.caution: No significant toxicity is recorded for Cornus mas. The ripe fruit flesh is safe to eat. The pit (stone) inside each drupe should not be chewed or eaten; simply spit it out or strain it out during cooking. Eat only fully ripe (deep red, soft) fruit — underripe cornelian cherries are very astringent and unpleasant. Do not confuse with ornamental dogwoods (Cornus florida, Cornus kousa) whose fruits are mildly toxic; Cornus mas fruits are distinctly olive-shaped and ripened to a solid deep red, quite unlike any toxic look-alike.[1][2]
